title: John the Baptizer — Part 1
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
series: The First Year of Jesus' Ministry
source_files: John the Baptizer - Part 1 - Matthew 3_1-6 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: The Historical Setting: When Did John Minister?
subpoints: The first year of Jesus's ministry is the year of obscurity, studied primarily from the Gospel of John chapters 1-4
Jesus was born around 5 BC; John the Baptist began his ministry in early AD 26 in the spring; Jesus was baptized in fall AD 26; the crucifixion occurred in spring AD 30
John and Jesus are eternally united in their proclamation: 'Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand' was the first sermon of both
John's ministry is so connected with Jesus's that you cannot study one without the other — John's ministry set up what Jesus was going to do

outline.title: Who John Was: The Prophesied Forerunner
subpoints: John came preaching in the wilderness of Judea — Isaiah 40:3 and Malachi 3:1 prophesied this forerunner who would prepare the way of the Lord
Matthew quotes Isaiah 40:3, and Mark refers to both Malachi 3:1 and Isaiah 40:3 under Isaiah's name, following ancient practice of citing the larger work
John's clothing — camel's hair with a leather belt — deliberately echoes Elijah's description in 2 Kings 1:8; John is the Elijah who was to come
John is not a theological Baptist — the title 'the Baptist' means 'the one who baptizes'; if his title reflected theology, he would be John the Presbyterian
Peter confirmed in Acts 1:21-22 that the gospel of Jesus begins with John's ministry — his work is inseparable from Christ's

outline.title: John's Message: Repent, for the Kingdom Is at Hand
subpoints: Repentance is the first word of John's ministry and of Jesus's ministry — it is the key that opens the gate to the kingdom of heaven
Repentance means a change of mind that produces a change of direction — turning from sin toward God
John's audience: Jerusalem, all Judea, and all the region around the Jordan went out to him and were baptized, confessing their sins
The entire region was catching John's fire — John was preparing everyone to see the Lamb of God who was about to manifest himself

outline.title: John's Baptism: What It Was and Was Not
subpoints: John's baptism was a sign of conviction of sin and repentance — it was not Christian baptism; Paul in Acts 19 had to re-baptize disciples who had only received John's baptism
John's baptism was unique to his transitional ministry — it prepared people to see the Lamb of God but did not confer what Christian baptism confers
The response of the whole region to John's preaching shows the power of a Spirit-empowered call to repentance
John's work was complete when he pointed to Jesus: 'Behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world'

summary:
Beginning a study of John the Baptist's ministry within the ongoing series on the first year of Jesus's ministry, Pastor Tito Lyro preaches from Matthew 3:1-6. The sermon opens with a brief chronological orientation: Jesus was born around 5 BC, John began his ministry in the spring of AD 26, Jesus was baptized in the fall of that year, and the crucifixion occurred in AD 30. John's ministry is so inseparably connected with Jesus's that the series on Jesus's first year must begin with John — and Peter confirmed this in Acts 1:21-22, saying that the replacement for Judas must be someone who was a witness from the baptism of John through the resurrection.

The sermon establishes John as the fulfillment of both Isaiah 40:3 and Malachi 3:1 — the voice in the wilderness who would prepare the way of the Lord and the forerunner who would precede the Lord's coming. John's camel-hair clothing and leather belt deliberately echo Elijah's description in 2 Kings 1:8, marking him as the Elijah-figure prophesied in Malachi 4. The sermon also clarifies that John's title 'the Baptist' is not a theological designation but a descriptive one — he is called that because he was the one who baptized; if his theology were being described, he would be John the Presbyterian.

The heart of the sermon is John's message: 'Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand.' This was the first word of John's ministry and also of Jesus's own first sermon. Repentance is the key that opens the gate to the kingdom of heaven. The entire region of Judea was caught up in John's preaching — Jerusalem, all Judea, and all the region around the Jordan came out and were baptized, confessing their sins. John's baptism was a sign of conviction of sin and repentance, not to be confused with Christian baptism (as Paul's encounter with Apollos's disciples in Acts 19 makes clear).
transcript:
## Scripture Reading: Matthew 3:1-6

If you're able, would you remain standing for the reading of God's Word? We're turning to Matthew chapter 3, and we're going to read verses 1 through 6 as we continue in our series on the first year of the life of Christ. There should be about nineteen sermons in this series, and we're going to do morning and afternoon, just working our way through that way.

Matthew chapter 3, starting at verse 1, reading through verse 6. This is the Word of our Lord.

*In those days John the Baptist came preaching in the wilderness of Judea, and saying, "Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand!" For this is he who was spoken of by the prophet Isaiah, saying: "The voice of one crying in the wilderness: 'Prepare the way of the Lord; make His paths straight.'" Now John himself was clothed in camel's hair, with a leather belt around his waist; and his food was locusts and wild honey. Then Jerusalem, all Judea, and all the region around the Jordan went out to him and were baptized by him in the Jordan, confessing their sins.*

This is the Word of our Lord. Let us pray together. Father in heaven, thank you that you've given us your word. We pray that Your Spirit would grab hold of Your word and apply it to our hearts this morning. We're asking in Jesus' name. Amen. Please be seated.

## Introduction: John and Jesus Are Inseparably United

I forgot to tell my helper that I won't need her help today — so you can relax. I had planned an illustration that just didn't come through.

John the Baptist and Jesus are eternally united in their proclamation: *Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand.* That was John's first message — his first sermon. It was Jesus's first sermon as well: repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand.

As I said earlier, we're studying the first year of Jesus's ministry. With John's ministry so connected with Jesus's ministry, you cannot study one without the other. John's ministry really set up what Jesus was going to do — so that's why we're spending a couple of lessons on John, and then transitioning next week, the Lord willing, as we see John's testimony of Jesus and Jesus's baptism on the following Lord's Day.

So we begin studying the ministry of Jesus by actually considering John the baptizer.

## Chronological Orientation

We saw last week that Jesus had a three-year public ministry, likely starting in AD 26. We went through the reasons why Jesus was born before Christ — and you can listen to that lesson if you don't remember the reasons. We know this chronology of Jesus's life primarily because of the Passover feasts in the Gospel of John — John gives us enough data that we have to have at least three years of public ministry there.

So that being the case: what we're reading here about John happened in the spring of AD 26, with Jesus's ministry starting officially six months later with his baptism — in the fall of AD 26 — with the crucifixion happening in the spring of AD 30.

Now, "AD" stands for anno Domini — the year of the Lord. And this first year of the series that we're studying is the year of obscurity. You don't find a lot about it in Matthew, Mark, or Luke — but John spends four full chapters on it. That's why this series will mostly be about John 1 through 4, with sprinkling from Matthew, Mark, and Luke.

## John the Baptizer: Who He Was

In verse 1: *In those days John the Baptist came preaching in the wilderness of Judea.*

Now, John and Jesus are inseparable when it comes to the gospel of Jesus Christ. This is confirmed by the Apostle Peter in Acts 1:21-22. When the disciples are choosing a replacement for Judas, Peter says the replacement must be someone who was a witness beginning from the baptism of John through the resurrection and ascension of Christ. The beginning of the gospel is John's ministry. You cannot separate John's ministry from Jesus's ministry.

A comment about the title "the Baptist." John the Son of Zacharias and Elizabeth — cousin of Jesus, since Elizabeth was a cousin of Mary — became known as John the Baptist. I have actually been asked: if John was a Baptist, why are you a Presbyterian? Well, the title "Baptist" has nothing to do with theological persuasion. It refers to what he was known for: the one who baptized. A better title would be John the Baptizer. If we were giving him a title based on theological persuasion, we would call him John the Presbyterian.

## John as the Fulfillment of Prophecy

Verse 3 tells us: *For this is he who was spoken of by the prophet Isaiah, saying: "The voice of one crying in the wilderness: 'Prepare the way of the Lord; make His paths straight.'"*

Matthew here quotes Isaiah 40:3. If you compare this with Mark's Gospel, you'll see that Mark quotes both Malachi 3:1 and Isaiah 40:3, but refers to the combined citation simply as "Isaiah" — following an ancient practice of citing the larger book when quoting from related prophets. From these two quotations we see that John's ministry is the fulfillment of the most ancient promises God gave his people about the one who would come before the Messiah.

John is the voice in the wilderness of Isaiah — the one who cries out to prepare the path. And he is the forerunner of Malachi — the messenger who prepares the way before the Lord's coming. Both prophecies are fulfilled in him.

And look at John's clothing in verse 4: *Now John himself was clothed in camel's hair, with a leather belt around his waist; and his food was locusts and wild honey.* This is not casual description. The phrase "camel's hair with a leather belt around his waist" is almost exactly the description of Elijah in 2 Kings 1:8. Matthew is pointing us to the fact that John is the Elijah who was to come — the prophet whom Malachi said God would send before the great and dreadful day of the Lord.

So John is the fulfillment of Isaiah 40, Malachi 3, and Malachi 4 simultaneously. He is the long-awaited forerunner of the Messiah.

## John's Message: Repent, for the Kingdom Is at Hand

Look at verse 2: *Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand!*

This was the first word of John's ministry. This was also the first word of Jesus's ministry — Matthew will tell us that in chapter 4. Repentance is the key that opens the gate to the kingdom of heaven.

Repentance means a change of mind that produces a change of direction. It is not merely feeling sorry — it is turning from sin toward God. John is not calling people to a mood of sadness; he is calling them to a genuine transformation of orientation. Turn from your sins to God. The kingdom of God is near — the Messiah is about to appear. You need to be ready for him. And the way you get ready is by repenting.

And what was the response? Verses 5-6: *Then Jerusalem, all Judea, and all the region around the Jordan went out to him and were baptized by him in the Jordan, confessing their sins.*

The entire region was on fire. Jerusalem. All of Judea. All the region around the Jordan. They were going out to this strange man in the wilderness who wore camel hair and ate locusts, and they were being baptized in the Jordan, confessing their sins. There was a wide-scale awakening. The whole region was catching John's fire.

And in that way, John was preparing everyone to see the Lamb of God who was about to manifest himself. When Jesus appears the following week and John says: *Behold the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world* — that declaration had been prepared for by months of John's preaching about repentance. People were ready to see the one who could actually do something about their sin.

## John's Baptism

Now, John's baptism was a sign of conviction of sin and repentance. The whole region was confessing their sins and going through the waters of the Jordan.

But we need to be clear: this was not Christian baptism. There were disciples in Ephesus in Acts 19 who had only received John's baptism, and Paul had to re-baptize them with Christian baptism in the name of Jesus. John's baptism was unique to his transitional ministry — it prepared people to see the Lamb of God but did not confer what Christian baptism confers, which is union with Christ's death and resurrection.

Whatever John's baptism was, it was a sign of conviction of sin. And the whole region was catching John's fire. In that way John prepared everyone to see the Lamb of God — who was about to manifest himself and take away the sins of the world.

So John the baptizer came to prepare the way of the Christ, so that all flesh would see the salvation of God. He accomplished his mission. Christ has come. And to you, Christ says: repent and enter my kingdom. Have you done that? Are you willing to do that? And are you willing to live a life of repentance?

Let us pray together. Father, we thank you for the faithful ministry of John the Baptist. We thank you for the preparation he provided for the way of our Savior. We thank you that our Savior has come. We pray that we would take heed to the message of repentance and that we would live a life of repentance. We're asking in Jesus' name. Amen.
